title: Kouign-amann
subtitle: Breton caramelised butter and sugar cake with a yeasted dough
excerpt: A yeasted dough made with bread flour is rolled out and layered with cold butter and sugar in three folds, then baked in a high-sided tin. The sugar caramelises against the tin and seeps through the layers during baking, creating a crisp, lacquered crust with a tender, laminated interior. The name is Breton for butter cake.
sourceNotes: Kouign-amann (pronounced kween ah-mahn) was created in 1860 by Yves-René Scordia, a baker in Douarnenez in Brittany, according to the most widely cited account. The name is Breton for butter cake. The recipe was originally improvised from bread dough folded with the surplus butter and sugar Brittany was known for. The lamination is simpler than croissant dough: fewer folds, no resting between each, and sugar is incorporated alongside the butter so it caramelises in the tin. The cake reached wider British attention through food writing in the 2000s and 2010s.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Kouign-amann is best eaten on the day it is made while the crust is still crisp. It can be gently rewarmed in the oven the following day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 180°C. The sugar caramelises against the tin in the final minutes; watch through the oven door and remove when the top is deep amber. Invert onto a rack immediately so the caramel does not set in the tin.

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm round tin, buttered and sugared

glossaryTerms.definition: A simplified butter lamination applied to a yeasted dough: cold butter is pressed flat and folded into the dough with sugar incorporated at the same time, in three folds with no resting between. The technique is far quicker than croissant lamination (which uses many more folds with resting periods) but produces a similar layered effect. The sugar distributed through the folds caramelises during baking and seeps through the layers, creating the characteristic crust.

content.text: that is quicker than croissant work and does not require overnight chilling. The dough is made and rested for one hour, then the butter and sugar are folded in over three turns without resting between each. The folded dough goes straight into the tin for a short final prove before baking. Turn the finished cake out immediately; if the caramel is left to set in the tin it will be impossible to remove.

attrs.prepNote: cold, for lamination, plus extra for the tin
plus 1 tbsp extra for the tin
to scatter on top before baking

content.text: Mix the flour, yeast, salt, and water together to a smooth dough. Knead for 8 minutes until elastic. Cover and leave to rest at room temperature for 1 hour.

Pound the cold butter between two sheets of baking paper into a flat square about 15 cm across. Roll the rested dough out to a rectangle about 30 × 20 cm. Place the butter square in the centre and fold the dough over it like an envelope. Scatter a third of the sugar over the dough. Roll out to the rectangle again, fold into thirds (letter fold), scatter another third of the sugar, and repeat once more for the third fold with the final sugar. The butter and sugar will smear and some may break through; press it back in and continue.

Butter a 20 cm round tin generously and dust with 1 tablespoon of caster sugar. Press the folded dough into the tin, spreading it to fill the base. Scatter the sea salt flakes over the top. Cover loosely and leave to prove for 20 minutes. Heat the oven to 200°C conventional (180°C fan). Bake for 30 to 35 minutes until deep amber on top. Invert onto a cooling rack immediately and leave the tin over the cake for 1 minute to let the caramel drip down. Eat warm.
